content: The warming of Earth’s climate is fundamentally driven by the enhanced greenhouse effect, wherein increased atmospheric concentrations of radiatively active gases—primarily CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O—amplify the natural infrared opacity of the troposphere. These gases absorb terrestrial longwave radiation emitted by the Earth’s surface and re-radiate it isotropically, effectively trapping heat and reducing net outgoing longwave radiation to space. Quantitatively, the radiative forcing from elevated CO₂, now exceeding 420 ppm, surpasses preindustrial levels by over 2 W/m², disrupting Earth’s radiative equilibrium. This perturbation initiates a cascade of feedbacks, including water vapor amplification and albedo changes from cryospheric retreat, which further intensify warming. Climate models robustly attribute the observed global mean surface temperature increase—approximately 1.1°C since the late 19th century—to anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions, corroborated by multiple lines of evidence including isotopic signatures, stratospheric cooling trends, and paleoclimate reconstructions. Thus, the fundamental physical mechanism linking greenhouse gas accumulation to climate warming is well-established and quantitatively constrained.
